1: Maddûaʿ miššadday lōʾ-niṣpĕnû ʿittîm: 'Why does the Almighty not set appointed times?' — ʿēt (time, appointed moment): the theodicy question framed temporally. The God of history (who by definition controls ʿittîm) appears not to have scheduled judgment for visible wickedness. Yōḏĕʿāyw lōʾ-ḥāzû yāmāyw: 'those who know him cannot see his days of reckoning' — the hiddenness of divine judgment as the existential problem.
2: Gĕbûlôt yassîgû: 'They move boundary stones' — gĕḇûl (boundary, territorial marker): the stones that demarcated tribal allotments. Moving them is the paradigmatic covenant violation (Deut 19:14; 27:17): it dissolves the land-distribution that expressed covenant justice. ʿēḏer gāzĕlû wayirʿû: 'they steal flocks and pasture them' — from gāzal (to seize by force): organized agricultural theft.
3: Ḥămôr yĕtōmîm yinḥāgû: 'They drive away the orphan's donkey' — the orphan's donkey is his means of livelihood; taking it is destroying his subsistence. Yāḥăbĕlû šôr ʾalmānāh: 'they seize the widow's ox as collateral' — ḥābal (to take as pledge): the covenant-protection violation of ch22:6 now listed as systemic practice.
4: Yaṭṭû ʾeḇyônîm midderek: 'They shove the needy off the road' — ʾeḇyôn (needy, poor): the standard covenant-protection category. ʿAniyyê-ʾāreṣ nistarrû yaḥad: 'the poor of the land go into hiding together' — the victims' response to the predatory society. Job is cataloguing the injustice that the friends say God always punishes immediately.
5: Hēn pĕrāʾîm bammiḏbār: 'Like wild donkeys in the desert' — pereʾ (wild donkey): the animal of the wilderness, untamed and desperately foraging. The poor are reduced to animal-level survival. Yāṣĕʾû pāʿălām lĕšaḥēr ṭāreP: 'they go out to work, seeking food' — the predator-prey language applied to the economic system.
6: Baśśāḏeh bĕlîlô yiqṣōrû: 'They harvest crops from fields that are not theirs' — the gleaning-rights of the poor violated by the powerful who harvest first. Kerem rāšāʿ yĕlaqqēšû: 'they glean in the wicked man's vineyard' — lāqaš (to gather late harvest): the destitute survive only by gleaning in fields controlled by those who oppress them.
7: ʿĀrôm yālînû mibbelî lĕḇûš: 'They spend the night naked, without garment' — the nakedness violation is both literal and covenantal: the law specifically protects the garment-collateral of the poor (Exod 22:26-27; Deut 24:12-13). Ûʾên kĕsût bassimrāh: 'no covering against the cold' — qōr (cold): exposed in winter weather.
8: Mizzereṃ hārîm yirṭābû: 'They are soaked by the mountain rains' — the weather-exposure of the homeless poor. Ûmibbelî māḥaseh ḥibbĕqû-ṣûr: 'they press against the rock for lack of shelter' — māḥaseh (shelter, refuge): the standard term for divine protection (Ps 46:2; 61:4) denied them by human wickedness.
9: Yigrĕzû miššaḏ yātôm: 'They snatch the infant from its mother's breast' — the ultimate violation: tearing nursing infants from their mothers, presumably for debt-slavery. Wĕʿal-ʿānî yaḥbōlû: 'they take collateral from the destitute' — the systematic ḥābal-extraction targeting the most vulnerable.
10: ʿĂrôm hālĕkû bĕlî lĕḇûš: 'They go about without clothing' — the workers who produce the harvest cannot afford to clothe themselves from its proceeds. Wĕrāʿēb nāśĕʾû ʿāmîr: 'starving, they carry the harvest sheaves' — the bitter irony: those who produce food go hungry.
11: Bên-šûrōtām yaṣhîrû: 'They press olives among the terrace walls' — the agricultural labor vocabulary. Yĕqāḇîm dārĕkû wayyiṣmāʾû: 'they tread the winepresses, yet go thirsty' — the vintage-labor paradox: processing wine for others while thirsting themselves.
12: Mēʿîr mĕtîm yinʾāqû: 'From the city the dying groan' — nāʾaq (to groan in death-agony): the pre-mortem cry. Wĕnepeš ḥălālîm tĕšawwēaʿ: 'the mortally wounded cry out' — šāwaʿ (to cry for help): the urgent distress call. Wĕʾĕlôah lōʾ-yāśîm tiplāh: 'yet God lays no charge of wrongdoing' — the theodicy punch: God sees, yet issues no indictment. This is Job's unresolved problem throughout the chapter.
13: Hēmmāh hāyû bĕmōrĕḏê-ʾôr: 'These are among those who rebel against the light' — mārad (to rebel): the rebellion-vocabulary of covenant violation applied to those who love darkness (cf. John 3:19-20). Lōʾ yāḏĕʾû dĕrākāyw wĕlōʾ yāšăḇû: 'they do not know its ways and will not walk its paths' — yāḏaʿ as covenantal knowledge refused.
14: Lāʾôr yāqûm haṣṣôrēaḥ: 'The murderer rises before dawn' — using the dark for cover, the criminal inverts the natural order: active when others sleep, hiding when others work. Ballaylāh yihyeh kagannāb: 'in the night he becomes the thief' — the convergence of murder and theft as darkness-crimes.
15: Wĕʿên nōpep yāšîm sēter pānāyw: 'No one will spot me; he disguises his face' — the adulterer's calculation of privacy. Šemar nešep ʿên tĕšûrēnî: 'watches for the twilight' — nešep (twilight, dusk): the ambiguous light that enables concealment.
16: Baḥōšeḵ yaḥtĕrû ḇāttîm: 'In the darkness they break through walls' — ḥātar (to dig through, break in): the thief's technique (cf. Matt 24:43). Yômām ḥittĕmû lāmô: 'by day they stay shut away' — their lives inverted: hidden by day, active by night.
17: Kî yaḥdāw bōqer lāhem ṣalmāwet: 'The shadow of death is their morning' — the criminals' temporal experience inverted: morning (when others wake to life) is their deep darkness. Yakkîr ballĕhôt ṣalmāwet: 'they are familiar with the horrors of death's darkness' — they are habituated to what others flee from.
18: Qal-hûʾ ʿal-pĕnê-mayim: 'He skims the surface of water like foam' — the transience of the wicked. Tĕqullal ḥelqātô bāʾāreṣ: 'his portion in the land is under a curse' — qālal (to be cursed/treated lightly): the curse that will ultimately overtake the wicked. Lōʾ-yipen derek kĕrāmîm: 'he does not turn toward vineyards' — perhaps prosperity escapes him.
19: Ṣiyāh wĕḥum yiśgĕlû mê-šeleg: 'drought and heat evaporate the snow-waters' — the comparison: just as the desert heat absorbs snow-melt instantly and completely, Šĕʾôl ḥāṭĕʾû: 'Sheol takes those who have sinned' — the same inevitable, total absorption. The movement from life to death is as natural and irresistible as evaporation.
20: Yiškĕḥēhû reḥem: 'The womb that bore him forgets him' — the ultimate erasure: the mother who bore him forgets him. Rimmāh timetqā-lô: 'the worm devours him eagerly' — rimmāh (worm, maggot): the post-mortem destruction. Lōʾ-yizzāḵēr ʿôḏ: 'he is no longer remembered' — the double erasure: forgotten by the living, consumed by worms.
21: Yirʿeh ʿăqārāh lōʾ-tēlēḏ: 'He preys on the childless woman who cannot conceive' — targeting those without social protection or descendants to advocate for them. Wĕʾalmānāh lōʾ-yēṭîb: 'he shows no kindness to the widow' — the widow and barren woman: the most socially vulnerable, without male protectors.
22: Weyimsōḵ ʾabbîrîm bĕḵōḥô: 'By his power he brings even the strong man down' — the tyrant who topples even those with resources. Yāqûm wĕlōʾ-yaʾămîn bĕḥayyāyw: 'he rises up, and no one's life is secure' — a difficult verse, possibly describing the tyrant's insecurity rather than his power.
23: Yittĕn-lô lābeṭaḥ wĕyiššāʿēn: 'God allows him to rest in security' — the theological crux: divine provision of security to the wicked. Wĕʿênāyw ʿal-darkêhem: 'yet God's eyes are on their every path' — the tension: security granted while divine surveillance continues. Job observes the complexity of divine providential action without forcing it into simple categories.
24: Rāmû mĕʿaṭ wĕʾênennû: 'They are lifted up for a moment and then they are gone' — the temporary exaltation of the wicked, followed by sudden removal. Kĕrōʾš šibbōlet yimmālû: 'they sink down like the top of a grain-stalk' — the grain-stalk bends and is cut: the image of swift, total harvesting.
25: Wĕʾim-lōʾ ʾēpôh mî yaḵzîḇēnî: 'And if this is not true, who will prove me wrong?' — Job's challenge to the friends: his observation of the world's moral complexity cannot be refuted. Wĕyāśēm lĕ-ʾepes millātî: 'who will show that my words are worthless?' — ʾepes (nothingness): Job's confidence that his account of reality is true.
